title: Phase-dependent role of dissipation across the Aubry-André-Harper transition
authors: Gerardo Suárez, Baptiste Debecker, Francesco Cosco, Francesco Plastina, François Damanet, Nicola Lo Gullo, Francesco Perciavalle
abstract: We study transport across the Aubry-André-Harper localization transition in the presence of non-Markovian dissipation. For a single particle initially at the center of the chain, we show that bath memory (i.e., finite decay time of bath correlations) plays distinct roles in the two phases. In the extended phase, bath memory qualitatively reshapes the dynamical generator, thereby producing transport patterns that cannot be reduced to a simple rescaling of time. By contrast, in the localized phase, the bath activates motion between localized states and bath memory mainly renormalizes the dynamical timescales. Our results identify localization as a simple filter of non-Markovian effects: memory restructures transport in the extended regime, but survives mainly as a timescale renormalization in the deeply localized regime.
summary: This paper explores how non-Markovian noise affects transport in the Aubry-André-Harper model. It demonstrates that while bath memory can fundamentally restructure transport in the delocalized phase, it only rescales the timing of transport in the localized phase. This identifies localization as a mechanism to filter out complex non-Markovian effects.
main_problem: The study investigates how non-Markovian dissipation and bath memory affect transport dynamics across the Aubry-André-Harper (AAH) localization transition.
main_result: The authors find that the localized phase acts as a filter: in the extended phase, bath memory qualitatively reshapes transport patterns, whereas in the localized phase, memory effects primarily act as a renormalization of dynamical timescales.
method: The study employs the Hierarchical Equations of Motion (HEOM) to capture non-Markovian dynamics and compares the results with Markovian (Lindblad/Bloch-Redfield) and semiclassical master equation approaches.
model_or_system: A single spinless fermion in a 1D quasiperiodic AAH chain coupled to a bosonic bath at a central site, where the bath is modeled with an exponentially decaying correlation function.
key_observables: Root-Mean-Square Displacement (RMSD), Population Participation Ratio (PPR), population dynamics, and the spectrum of the dynamical generator.
important_parameters: Disorder strength (h), coupling strength (kappa), dissipation rate (gamma), bath frequency (omega_0), and the localization-delocalization transition point (h_c = 2J).
main_assumptions: The localized regime analysis assumes a deeply localized limit where eigenstates are approximately site-localized and uses a truncated hierarchy for HEOM.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic setup and the qualitative difference in transport; Figure 2 displays population dynamics and RMSD/PPR scaling across the transition; Supplemental figures compare HEOM with semiclassical and Markovian methods and show spectral clustering.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the AAH model and non-Markovian setup, presents the numerical HEOM results for transport in both phases, provides a theoretical derivation of the semiclassical master equation for the localized regime, and concludes with a comparison of different dynamical generators.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it reveals how environmental memory can be controlled or filtered by the underlying localization properties of a system, suggesting potential for bath engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Cartoon of the setup: a fermionic quasiperiodic chain is locally coupled at its central site to a bosonic bath, modeled as a non-Markovian environment with exponentially decaying correlations C(τ) and decay rate γ. Transport is controlled by system–bath parameters. In the extended phase, γ sets the spreading profile, uniform for large γ and non-uniform for small γ. In the localized phase, dissipation in- duces incoherent hopping, with γ mainly fixing the timescale rather than the spatial profile.

Figure 2. Dynamics of a single particle in a dissipative AAH chain across the extended–localized transition. Panels (a) and (b) show the population dynamics in the extended phase (h = 1) and in the localized phase (h = 10), respectively, each for three different values of γ. Panels (c)–(f) display the RMSD, with the PPR shown in the insets, across the phase transition for different values of h. In each panel different values of γ are considered, as indicated by the color bars. In the localized phase, time is rescaled as ˜t = Γeff t, where Γeff = κγ2

Figure 3. Features of the HEOM spectrum across the extended-to-localized transition. (a) Real parts of LHEOM eigenvalues, showing clustering that identifies ∆HEOM, τ −1 fast, and τ −1 slow. Parameters: L = 15, γ = 2, tier= 6. (b) Real and imaginary parts of LHEOM eigenvalues in the localized phase for different h and L = 33, zoomed on fast and slow clusters. Parameters: γ = 2, tier= 6. For both panels (a) and (b), we consider the L2 eigenvalues of LHEOM with the largest real parts. (c),(d) ∆HEOM and τ −1 slow vs h in the localized phase for various γ. L = 15; tier= 8 (γ = 1), tier= 6 (γ = 2, 3), tier= 5 (γ = 4, 5). In all panels, κ = 2.
